mn13:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn13:10.1: And if, bhikkhus, while that man of family is thus striving, exerting himself, and making an effort, those possessions do come to fruition,
mn13:10.2: he experiences *dukkha* and distress on account of protecting those possessions:
mn13:10.3: ‘How can it be that kings do not seize my possessions, thieves do not seize them, fire does not burn them, water does not carry them away, and unloved heirs do not take them?’
mn13:10.4: While he is thus guarding and protecting those possessions, kings seize them, or thieves seize them, or fire burns them, or water carries them away, or unloved heirs take them.
mn13:10.5: He sorrows, grows weary, laments, beats his breast and cries out, and falls into confusion:
mn13:10.6: ‘What I had is now no more.’
mn13:10.7: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures, visible here and now, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
mn13:11.1: Again, bhikkhus, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause, kings quarrel with kings, nobles quarrel with nobles, brahmins quarrel with brahmins, householders quarrel with householders, mother quarrels with son, son quarrels with mother, father quarrels with son, son quarrels with father, brother quarrels with brother, brother quarrels with sister, sister quarrels with brother, friend quarrels with friend.
mn13:11.2: There, having fallen into quarrels, disputes, and arguments, they attack one another with fists, they attack one another with clods, they attack one another with sticks, they attack one another with weapons.
mn13:11.3: There they meet with death, or deadly *dukkha*.
mn13:11.4: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures, visible here and now, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
mn13:12.1: Again, bhikkhus, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause, taking up sword and shield, fastening on bow and quiver, they charge into battle with both sides drawn up, while arrows are being shot, spears are being hurled, and swords are flashing.
mn13:12.2: There they are pierced by arrows, they are pierced by spears, and their heads are cut off by swords.
mn13:12.3: There they meet with death, or deadly *dukkha*.
mn13:12.4: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures, visible here and now, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
mn13:13.1: Again, bhikkhus, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause, taking up sword and shield, fastening on bow and quiver, they storm ramparts plastered with wet clay, while arrows are being shot, spears are being hurled, and swords are flashing.
mn13:13.2: There they are pierced by arrows, they are pierced by spears, they are doused with dung, they are crushed with heavy blows, and their heads are cut off by swords.
mn13:13.3: There they meet with death, or deadly *dukkha*.
mn13:13.4: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures, visible here and now, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
mn13:14.1: Again, bhikkhus, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause, they break into houses, plunder goods, commit burglary, lie in ambush on the road, and go to other people’s wives.
mn13:14.2: Kings have them arrested and inflict various punishments on them—
mn13:14.3: they flog them with whips, they flog them with canes, they flog them with clubs; they cut off their hands, they cut off their feet, they cut off their hands and feet, they cut off their ears, they cut off their noses, they cut off their ears and noses; they subject them to the bowl-shell, the conch-shell shave, Rāhu’s mouth, the fire-garland, the hand-torch, the grass-wrap, the bark-garment, the antelope, the flesh-hooks, the coin-cutting, the lye-scraping, the bar-turning, the straw-seat; they pour hot oil on them, they have them devoured by dogs, they impale them alive on stakes, and they cut off their heads with swords.
mn13:14.4: There they meet with death, or deadly *dukkha*.
mn13:14.5: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures, visible here and now, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
mn13:15.1: Again, bhikkhus, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause, they engage in bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct.
mn13:15.2: Having engaged in bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct, with the breakup of the body, after death, they are reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, ruin, hell.
mn13:15.3: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures pertaining to the hereafter, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
mn13:16.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the escape from sensual pleasures?
mn13:16.2: The removal of desire and lust for sensual pleasures, the giving up of desire and lust—this is the escape from sensual pleasures.
mn13:17.1: Whatever ascetics or brahmins, bhikkhus, do not truly understand as it actually is the gratification in sensual pleasures as gratification, the drawback as drawback, and the escape as escape—there is no possibility that they will themselves fully understand sensual pleasures, or that they will encourage another in such a way that, practicing accordingly, he will fully understand sensual pleasures.
mn13:17.2: But whatever ascetics or brahmins, bhikkhus, do truly understand as it actually is the gratification in sensual pleasures as gratification, the drawback as drawback, and the escape as escape—there is the possibility that they will themselves fully understand sensual pleasures, or that they will encourage another in such a way that, practicing accordingly, he will fully understand sensual pleasures.
mn13:18.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the gratification in forms?
mn13:18.2: Suppose, bhikkhus, there were a noble girl, or a brahmin girl, or a householder’s girl, fifteen or sixteen years old, neither too tall nor too short, neither too thin nor too fat, neither too dark nor too fair. Would she not then be at the height of beauty and lovely appearance?
mn13:18.4: Whatever *sukha* and gladness arise dependent on beauty and lovely appearance—
mn13:18.5: this is the gratification in forms.
mn13:19.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the drawback in forms?
mn13:19.2: Here, bhikkhus, one might see that same woman at another time, eighty, ninety, or a hundred years old, aged, bent like a roof-beam, crooked, leaning on a stick, trembling as she goes, afflicted, her youth gone, with broken teeth, grey-haired, scanty-haired, bald-headed, wrinkled, her limbs blotched with spots.
mn13:19.4: Has the former beauty and lovely appearance vanished, and has the drawback become evident?”
mn13:19.6: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in forms.
mn13:2.1: Then several *bhikkhus*, in the morning, having dressed and taken their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms.
mn13:2.3: “It is still too early to wander for alms in Sāvatthī.
mn13:2.4: Why don’t we go to the park of the wanderers of other sects?”
mn13:2.5: Then those *bhikkhus* went to the park of the wanderers of other sects. Having gone there, they exchanged greetings with those wanderers of other sects.
mn13:2.6: After conducting a cordial and memorable conversation, they sat down to one side. When they were seated to one side, those wanderers of other sects said this to those *bhikkhus*:
mn13:20.1: Again, bhikkhus, one might see that same woman sick, suffering, gravely ill, lying fouled in her own urine and excrement, being उठ? no, raised up by others and laid down by others.
mn13:20.3: Has the former beauty and lovely appearance vanished, and has the drawback become evident?”
mn13:20.5: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in forms.
mn13:21.1: Again, bhikkhus, one might see that same woman’s body thrown aside in a charnel ground—
mn13:21.2: dead for one day, or two days, or three days, bloated, livid, and oozing pus.
mn13:21.4: Has the former beauty and lovely appearance vanished, and has the drawback become evident?”
mn13:21.6: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in forms.
mn13:22.1: Again, bhikkhus, one might see that same woman’s body thrown aside in a charnel ground—
mn13:22.2: being eaten by crows, or being eaten by kites, or being eaten by vultures, or being eaten by falcons, or being eaten by dogs, or being eaten by tigers, or being eaten by leopards, or being eaten by jackals, or being eaten by various kinds of creatures.
mn13:22.3: What do you think, bhikkhus? Has the former beauty and lovely appearance vanished, and has the drawback become evident?”
mn13:22.5: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in forms.
mn13:23-28.1: Again, bhikkhus, one might see that same woman’s body thrown aside in a charnel ground—
mn13:23-28.2: a skeleton with flesh and blood, held together by tendons; a skeleton without flesh but smeared with blood, held together by tendons; a skeleton without flesh and blood, held together by tendons; bones disconnected, scattered in all directions—
mn13:23-28.3: here a hand-bone, there a foot-bone, here an ankle-bone, there a shin-bone, here a thigh-bone, there a hip-bone, here a rib-bone, there a spine-bone, here a shoulder-bone, there a neck-bone, here a jaw-bone, there a tooth, here a skull.
mn13:23-28.4: What do you think, bhikkhus? Has the former beauty and lovely appearance vanished, and has the drawback become evident?”
mn13:23-28.6: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in forms.
mn13:29.1: Again, bhikkhus, one might see that same woman’s body thrown aside in a charnel ground—
mn13:29.2: bones white, the color of shells; bones piled up, more than a year old; bones rotten and reduced to powder.
mn13:29.4: Has the former beauty and lovely appearance vanished, and has the drawback become evident?”
mn13:29.6: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in forms.
mn13:3.1: “Friends, the ascetic Gotama declares the full understanding of sensual pleasures, and we too declare the full understanding of sensual pleasures.
mn13:3.2: Friends, the ascetic Gotama declares the full understanding of forms, and we too declare the full understanding of forms.
mn13:3.3: Friends, the ascetic Gotama declares the full understanding of *vedanā*s, and we too declare the full understanding of *vedanā*s.
mn13:3.4: So what distinction is there here, friends, what superiority, what difference between the ascetic Gotama and us—
mn13:3.5: that is, between a teaching of *dhamma* [*dhammadesanāya*] and a teaching of *dhamma* [*dhammadesanaṁ*], or between an instruction and an instruction?”
mn13:30.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the escape from forms?
mn13:30.2: The removal of desire and lust for forms, the giving up of desire and lust—this is the escape from forms.
mn13:31.1: Whatever ascetics or brahmins, bhikkhus, do not truly understand as it actually is the gratification in forms as gratification, the drawback as drawback, and the escape as escape—there is no possibility that they will themselves fully understand forms, or that they will encourage another in such a way that, practicing accordingly, he will fully understand forms.
mn13:31.2: But whatever ascetics or brahmins, bhikkhus, do truly understand as it actually is the gratification in forms as gratification, the drawback as drawback, and the escape as escape—there is the possibility that they will themselves fully understand forms, or that they will encourage another in such a way that, practicing accordingly, he will fully understand forms.
mn13:32.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the gratification in *vedanā*s?
mn13:32.2: Here, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakkaṁ*] and examination [*vicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn13:32.3: At a time when a *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakkaṁ*] and examination [*vicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*], at that time he does not intend his own affliction, he does not intend the affliction of another, he does not intend the affliction of both.
mn13:32.4: At that time he feels only unafflicting *vedanā*.
mn13:32.5: I say that this freedom from affliction is the highest gratification of *vedanā*s.
mn13:33-35.1: Again, bhikkhus, with the stilling of thought [*vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā*] and examination [*vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā*], a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, which has internal confidence and unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind, without thought [*avitakkaṁ*] and without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijaṁ*] …
mn13:33-35.2: at a time when, bhikkhus, with the fading of *pīti* [*pītiyā ca virāgā*], a *bhikkhu* dwells in equanimity [*upekkhako*], with *sati* [*sato*] and full awareness [*sampajāno*], and experiences *sukha* with the body, entering and dwelling in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones declare: ‘equanimous [*upekkhako*], with *sati* [*satimā*], dwelling in *sukha* [*sukhavihārī*]’ …
mn13:33-35.3: at a time when, bhikkhus, with the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the previous disappearance of gladness and distress, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant and is purified by equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*], at that time he does not intend his own affliction, he does not intend the affliction of another, he does not intend the affliction of both.
mn13:33-35.4: At that time he feels only unafflicting *vedanā*.
mn13:33-35.5: I say that this freedom from affliction is the highest gratification of *vedanā*s.
mn13:36.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the drawback in *vedanā*s?
mn13:36.2: In that *vedanā*s are impermanent [*aniccā*], *dukkha*, and subject to change—this is the drawback in *vedanā*s.
mn13:37.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the escape from *vedanā*s?
mn13:37.2: The removal of desire and lust for *vedanā*s, the giving up of desire and lust—this is the escape from *vedanā*s.
mn13:38.1: Whatever ascetics or brahmins, bhikkhus, do not truly understand as it actually is the gratification in *vedanā*s as gratification, the drawback as drawback, and the escape as escape, there is no possibility that they will themselves fully understand *vedanā*, or that they will encourage another in such a way that, practicing accordingly, he will fully understand *vedanā*.
mn13:38.2: But whatever ascetics or brahmins, bhikkhus, do truly understand as it actually is the gratification in *vedanā*s as gratification, the drawback as drawback, and the escape as escape, there is the possibility that they will themselves fully understand *vedanā*, or that they will encourage another in such a way that, practicing accordingly, he will fully understand *vedanā*.”
mn13:38.4: Those *bhikkhus*, satisfied, approved of the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn13:38.5: The Greater Mass of *dukkha* is finished, the third.
mn13:4.1: Then those *bhikkhus* neither approved nor rejected what those wanderers of other sects had said.
mn13:4.2: Without approving and without rejecting it, they rose from their seats and departed, thinking:
mn13:4.3: “We will learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of the *Bhagavā*.”
mn13:5.1: Then those *bhikkhus*, after wandering for alms in Sāvatthī and returning from their almsround after the meal, went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to him, they paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn13:5.10: Friends, the ascetic Gotama declares the full understanding of *vedanā*s, and we too declare the full understanding of *vedanā*s.
mn13:5.11: So what distinction is there here, friends, what superiority, what difference between the ascetic Gotama and us—that is, between a teaching of *dhamma* [*dhammadesanāya*] and a teaching of *dhamma* [*dhammadesanaṁ*], or between an instruction and an instruction?’
mn13:5.12: Then, bhante, we neither approved nor rejected what those wanderers of other sects had said.
mn13:5.13: Without approving and without rejecting it, we rose from our seats and departed, thinking:
mn13:5.14: ‘We will learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of the *Bhagavā*.’”
mn13:5.2: “Here, bhante, in the morning, having dressed and taken our bowls and robes, we entered Sāvatthī for alms.
mn13:5.3: Then it occurred to us, bhante:
mn13:5.4: ‘It is still too early to wander for alms in Sāvatthī.
mn13:5.5: Why don’t we go to the park of the wanderers of other sects?’
mn13:5.6: Then, bhante, we went to the park of the wanderers of other sects. Having gone there, we exchanged greetings with those wanderers of other sects.
mn13:5.7: After conducting a cordial and memorable conversation, we sat down to one side. When we were seated to one side, bhante, those wanderers of other sects said this to us:
mn13:5.8: ‘Friends, the ascetic Gotama declares the full understanding of sensual pleasures, and we too declare the full understanding of sensual pleasures.
mn13:5.9: Friends, the ascetic Gotama declares the full understanding of forms, and we too declare the full understanding of forms.
mn13:6.1: “When wanderers of other sects speak like this, bhikkhus, they should be asked:
mn13:6.2: ‘But friends, what is the gratification in sensual pleasures, what is the drawback, and what is the escape?
mn13:6.3: What is the gratification in forms, what is the drawback, and what is the escape?
mn13:6.4: What is the gratification in *vedanā*s, what is the drawback, and what is the escape?’
mn13:6.5: When asked in this way, bhikkhus, those wanderers of other sects would not be able to answer, and beyond that they would fall into vexation.
mn13:6.7: Because, bhikkhus, this is outside their domain.
mn13:6.8: Bhikkhus, I do not see anyone in the world with its devatās, Māras, and Brahmās, in this generation with its ascetics and brahmins, its devas and humans, who could satisfy the mind by answering these questions—other than the Tathāgata, or a disciple of the Tathāgata, or one who has heard it from them.
mn13:7.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the gratification in sensual pleasures?
mn13:7.10: Whatever *sukha* and gladness arise dependent on these five cords of sensual pleasure—this is the gratification in sensual pleasures.
mn13:7.2: There are these five cords of sensual pleasure, bhikkhus.
mn13:7.4: Forms cognizable by the eye that are wished for, desired, agreeable, pleasing, connected with sensual pleasure, and enticing,
mn13:7.8: tangibles cognizable by the body that are wished for, desired, agreeable, pleasing, connected with sensual pleasure, and enticing—
mn13:7.9: these, bhikkhus, are the five cords of sensual pleasure.
mn13:8.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the drawback in sensual pleasures?
mn13:8.2: Here, bhikkhus, a man of family makes his living by some craft—
mn13:8.3: whether by accounting, or by calculation, or by reckoning, or by farming, or by trade, or by cattle-tending, or by archery, or by royal service, or by some other craft—
mn13:8.4: and he is afflicted by cold, afflicted by heat, afflicted by contact with gadflies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles, dying from hunger and thirst.
mn13:8.5: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures, visible here and now, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
mn13:9.1: And if, bhikkhus, while that man of family is thus striving, exerting himself, and making an effort, those possessions do not come to fruition,
mn13:9.2: he sorrows, grows weary, laments, beats his breast and cries out, and falls into confusion:
mn13:9.3: ‘My striving was futile! My effort was fruitless!’
mn13:9.4: This too, bhikkhus, is a drawback in sensual pleasures, visible here and now, a mass of *dukkha*, because of sensual pleasures, with sensual pleasures as the source, sensual pleasures as the basis, and sensual pleasures themselves as the cause.
